tone_and_voice: Urgent but disciplined -- humanitarian writing must convey severity without sensationalism, and must hold itself to Sphere/CHS accountability language rather than general advocacy language. Every finding should read as evidence for an operational decision, not a fundraising appeal.

section_rules.executive_summary: Must explicitly distinguish between interventions that met their standard (report as verified success) and interventions that partially or did not meet their standard (report as a specific, named accountability gap) -- never blend these into a single 'response was well-received' narrative.
section_rules.discussion: Must analyze WHY a gap occurred where evidence supports it (e.g. a specific supply chain delay), not just that it occurred -- CHS accountability requires explaining response failures, not only documenting them.
section_rules.methodology: State the rapid-assessment context explicitly (this is not a comprehensive needs assessment; note sample size and time constraints appropriate to the emergency phase).
section_rules.limitations: Must note if the assessment was conducted during active response (higher urgency, potentially lower methodological rigor) vs. post-response evaluation, since this affects appropriate confidence levels.
section_rules.recommendations: Every recommendation involving an unmet standard must include a specific, dated resolution commitment structure, not a general 'continue to monitor' statement -- CHS-aligned reporting requires accountability specificity.
section_rules.policy_implications: Rare in rapid needs assessments -- most findings are operational/response-level; only escalate to policy if a finding indicates a systemic response-system gap beyond this single response.
section_rules.lessons_learned: Should explicitly note methodological lessons for the NEXT rapid assessment (e.g. what worked about the data collection channel choice in this specific emergency context).
section_rules.conclusion: Must state plainly which Sphere/CHS standards were met, which were partially met, and which were not met -- avoid a single blended positive conclusion when specific gaps were identified.
section_rules.annexes: Include the specific humanitarian response phase (acute emergency, stabilization, early recovery) since Sphere standards and appropriate findings interpretation differ by phase.
section_rules.evidence_style: Every claim about standard achievement or shortfall must cite Sphere/CHS by the SPECIFIC standard or commitment number, never a blanket compliance claim.

common_risks: conflating a genuinely severe finding with a lower-severity operational complaint
reporting only positive findings due to time pressure in an active response
insufficient disaggregation by vulnerability group (e.g. elderly, disabled, female-headed households) in a rapid assessment
typical_recommendations: formal incident documentation against the specific Sphere standard not met
supply chain review at the specific delivery stage where delay occurred
specific, dated resolution commitments rather than general monitoring continuation
typical_policy_implications: response-system-level supply chain or pre-positioning policy
sector_knowledge.typical_donor_expectations: Humanitarian donors and CHS-committed agencies specifically expect TRANSPARENT reporting of shortfalls, not only successes -- a rapid needs assessment that reports only positive findings will be read as less credible, not more successful.

forbidden_behaviors: Never claim Sphere or CHS 'compliance' as a blanket statement -- always name the specific standard/commitment and whether it was met, partially met, or not met.
Never soften a documented shortfall (e.g. late shelter delivery) into ambiguous language to preserve an overall positive narrative -- CHS accountability requires transparent shortfall reporting.
Never present rapid-assessment findings with the same confidence level as a comprehensive, longer-fielded needs assessment.
